text: We begin by dissecting posteriorly along the visceral pleura.
This is then carried upwards around the apex of the hilum.
This allows us to identify the apicoanterior branch of the PA.
We then shift our attention back to the posterior hilum to help identify structures.
Here you can see us taking lymph node number 11 on the PA and subsequently sent it for frozen, which turned out to be negative.
We then dissected at the fissure and completed the posterior fissure with the staple load.
Once the fissure is opened, the posterior pulmonary artery, both ascending and descending branches are identified and taken with a robotic staple load.
We then turn our attention anteriorly where we have the superior pulmonary vein that is identified.
The superior pulmonary vein normally has three branches.
Shown in green is the vein that drains the apical and posterior segments, while the blue drains the lingular segment.
Not identified in this picture behind the fat is the vein that drains the anterior segment.
We then take the vein that drains the apical and posterior segments.
Once this is taken, you can identify the crotch between the apical and anterior segmental pulmonary artery branches as marked here.
In green, we have the apical branch and in blue, the anterior pulmonary artery.
We subsequently took the apical branch.
There's often a pulmonary artery that runs in the fissure that supplies the posterior segment.
This was found, verified it was not going to the lingula and taken with a vascular load.
This allowed visualization of the bronchus.
We dissected on the bronchus, removed the lymph node to identify the crotch where the anterior and apical posterior segments branch off.
In green is the apical posterior branch and in blue is the anterior branch.
This is then taken with the staple load.
To plan for resection, we have anesthesia give positive pressure to help identify the margins and begin to take it with the green loads.
We ultimately used two 12 millimeter ports to allow for stapling in multiple directions to avoid injuring other hilar structures.
We complete the operation by doing an intercostal nerve block, taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament, and our practice is to place tisial to help with hemostasis.
